title: The Switch That Must Not Surprise Anyone
context: The Expenses team is in a go-to-market design review for the first release of the new module. Revenue wants the feature visible everywhere to drive awareness. Payroll is worried that enabling Expenses will confuse customers who already use Claims. Engineering wants the fastest possible implementation path. You are the solution architect for the Expenses initiative. The VP of Product is chairing.

questions.prompt: The Revenue Director opens: "We should show the Expenses toggle to every admin account, even if they have not bought the module yet. Just grey it out and add a tooltip saying 'contact sales'. It is free advertising inside the product." The Sysadmin design lead looks uneasy. How should you respond?

options.text: That is a sensible compromise. It keeps the product discoverable, it avoids engineering work around entitlement checks, and a greyed-out toggle still prevents accidental enablement. Admins are used to seeing unavailable settings, so the confusion risk is low. We can add a confirmation modal later if we decide to make the toggle active for entitled customers.
options.explanation: This cuts directly against the requirement. The activation control is meant to be an unambiguous 'on switch' for customers who have purchased the module, while preventing accidental enablement for non-entitled customers. Surfacing the switch to everyone turns an entitlement control into a sales banner and makes the status less clear, not more clear.

options.text: I would keep the entitlement decision authoritative and keep the activation control for customers who have actually purchased the module. The requirement here is not product discovery; it is confident enablement with clear entitlement. If we want in-product upsell, we should solve that somewhere other than the actual admin switch that governs whether the module goes live.
options.explanation: This is the right response. It protects the core purpose of the feature: a clear, entitlement-based activation point that is only available to purchased customers. It also keeps the semantics clean for admins, which matters because the stated goal is confident enablement rather than teaser marketing.

options.text: We can show the toggle to everyone and rely on a warning banner plus a second confirmation step when they click it. That gives Sales the visibility they want while still protecting against accidental enablement. The entitlement check can happen after the admin expresses intent.
options.explanation: A second confirmation step does not solve the underlying problem. The requirement is that customers who have not purchased the module cannot switch it on. Making the control visible and then rejecting the action later still creates ambiguity and support noise around whether the customer is meant to have access.

questions.prompt: The Engineering Manager says: "The quickest way to ship is to reuse the existing Claims flag and just relabel the navigation. From the user's perspective they both deal with expenses anyway. We can separate them properly later." Payroll immediately asks whether Claims is being replaced. How should you respond?

options.text: That is acceptable for a first release because users care more about getting a working feature than about the internal module boundaries. If the UI makes it look like one consolidated experience, we can sort the entitlement split and the product naming out in a later release once adoption proves the case.
options.explanation: This breaks a core requirement. Expenses is explicitly additive and does not replace existing Claims capability. Reusing the Claims flag and relabelling the UI would create the exact confusion the PRD is trying to avoid, especially for customers and payroll users who already rely on the current Claims experience.

options.text: We should not collapse Claims and Expenses into one switch. The requirement is explicit that both modules remain available and that Expenses does not replace Claims. That means separate entitlement and activation semantics, plus clear user-facing language so customers understand they are enabling an additional module rather than renaming an existing one.
options.explanation: This is the right response. It protects the additive nature of the module and avoids a false simplification that would blur product boundaries. The architecture and the UI both need to respect that Claims continues to exist alongside Expenses.

options.text: We can reuse the Claims flag behind the scenes as long as we keep two menu entries in the UI. The implementation detail does not matter if the labels are different, and it would let us avoid a separate entitlement path for the first version.
options.explanation: The implementation detail does matter here because entitlement, visibility, and lifecycle behaviour are part of the requirement. If both modules are driven by the same flag, you no longer have a clean way to say that Claims remains available regardless of whether Expenses has been purchased and activated.

questions.prompt: The Payroll Manager asks bluntly: "When a customer enables Expenses, what changes for Payroll and Payroll Managers on day one? I do not want this module activation causing side effects in the existing payroll experience." The room goes quiet. How should you respond?

options.text: There will inevitably be some changes because Expenses and Payroll are closely related domains. Even if the goal is minimal disruption, we should expect at least some UI and process adjustments for payroll users once the module is enabled, especially as reimbursement data starts moving through the platform.
options.explanation: That answer contradicts the stated requirement. The module activation story explicitly says the experience and functionality for Payroll and Payroll Managers should remain the same. Whatever downstream integration work happens later, enablement itself is not meant to alter payroll users' day-one experience.

options.text: The activation requirement is designed specifically to avoid changing the current payroll experience. Enabling Expenses should make the new module visible to employees, but it should not alter Payroll or Payroll Manager workflows just because the toggle was turned on. If later phases pass data into payroll, that should be introduced through explicit downstream capability, not as a hidden side effect of activation.
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. It reassures Payroll using the exact intent of the requirement: activation should not disrupt existing payroll business-as-usual. It also cleanly separates module visibility from later integration behaviour.

options.text: We can minimise the disruption by adding a new payroll landing page that explains the Expenses module the first time payroll users log in after activation. That way they at least understand why the experience has changed and where to go for guidance.
options.explanation: This still assumes a change to the payroll experience, which the requirement says should not happen. Adding explanatory onboarding to payroll users may sound helpful, but it is solving a problem the module should not create in the first place.

questions.prompt: The Product Manager says: "Marketing wants a release point this quarter. Can we ship the on switch early, expose an empty Expenses shell, and then fill in the rest of the functionality over the following sprints? It would still count as progress." How should you respond?

options.text: Yes. The switch is independent enough to release on its own. Even if the rest of the module is not ready, getting customers used to seeing Expenses in the product is a good adoption tactic and gives Marketing something concrete to announce.
options.explanation: The sequencing notes do not support a customer-facing release of the switch by itself. They say development can start once the switch exists, but the customer will not see a release point until the whole piece of work has been completed. Shipping an empty shell would undermine the 'go live confidently' objective.

options.text: We can absolutely build and test the activation capability early behind internal controls, but I would not make it a customer-visible release point on its own. The stated plan is that customers do not see a release until the whole piece of work is complete. An empty shell would weaken trust because the switch would signal readiness without delivering the actual module experience.
options.explanation: This is the right response. It preserves internal sequencing flexibility while respecting the requirement that customers should not get a partial external release. The activation control is supposed to represent real readiness, not placeholder progress.

options.text: We should release the switch broadly but mark the module beta and unsupported. That reduces expectation-setting risk while still letting us learn from real customers before the rest of the functionality is done.
options.explanation: That still creates a customer-visible release point before the module is complete. The requirement is stricter than 'beta at your own risk'; it explicitly says customers will not see a release point until the overall work is completed.

questions.prompt: Customer Success raises a rollout idea: "Some customers will want to pilot this with one department before going wider. Can we make the first release enable Expenses for only a subset of employees?" The commercial team likes the sound of it. How should you respond?

options.text: Yes, departmental rollout should be part of the first release because controlled pilots are a common enterprise expectation. If we already know customers may ask for it, it is safer to design the activation model around employee segments rather than a single customer-level switch.
options.explanation: This goes beyond what the requirement says. The current activation story is simple: once the module is switched on, it is visible for all employees. Adding cohort-based rollout changes the semantics of the switch and adds scope that is not in the PRD.

options.text: For phase one I would keep activation at the customer level. The current requirement is that once the module is switched on it becomes visible for all employees, which keeps the behaviour predictable and easy for sysadmins to understand. If pilot cohorts become important later, that should be treated as a separate capability rather than hidden inside the initial on-switch design.
options.explanation: This is the correct answer because it protects the required post-enable behaviour: once enabled, Expenses appears for all employees as expected. It also keeps the activation model intentionally simple for the first release.

options.text: We can compromise by enabling the module for all employees but showing the navigation only to selected departments. That gives us a pilot without changing the activation semantics in the back end.
options.explanation: That is still a subset rollout, just implemented indirectly. The requirement is not just about technical activation; it is about the user-visible outcome being predictable. Hiding the module from some employees after activation would violate that.
